statutes.title: Pakistan Penal Code – Section 302
statutes.content: Section 302 PPC: Punishment of Qatl-i-Amd. Whoever commits qatl-i-amd shall, subject to the provisions of this Chapter be: (a) punished with death as Qisas; (b) punished with death or imprisonment for life as tazir having regard to the facts and circumstances of the case, if the proof in either of the forms specified in section 304 is not available.

statutes.title: Pakistan Penal Code – Section 420 (Cheating)
statutes.content: Section 420 PPC: Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property. Whoever cheats and thereby dishonestly induces the person deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to make, alter or destroy the whole or any part of a valuable security, or anything which is signed or sealed, and which is capable of being converted into a valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

statutes.title: Civil Procedure Code – Order I – Parties to Suit
statutes.content: Order I CPC deals with parties to suits. All persons may be joined as plaintiffs in whom any right to relief in respect of or arising out of the same act or transaction or series of acts or transactions is alleged to exist, whether jointly, severally or in the alternative. All persons may be joined as defendants against whom any right to relief in respect of or arising out of the same act or transaction or series of acts or transactions is alleged to exist.

statutes.title: Constitution of Pakistan – Article 10-A (Fair Trial)
statutes.content: Article 10-A: Right to fair trial. For the determination of his civil rights and obligations or in any criminal charge against him a person shall be entitled to a fair trial and due process.

statutes.title: Constitution of Pakistan – Article 25 (Equality)
statutes.content: Article 25: Equality of citizens. (1) All citizens are equal before law and are entitled to equal protection of law. (2) There shall be no discrimination on the basis of sex alone. (3) Nothing in this Article shall prevent the State from making any special provision for the protection of women and children.

statutes.title: Constitution of Pakistan – Article 184(3) (Original Jurisdiction)
statutes.content: Article 184(3): Without prejudice to the provisions of Article 199, the Supreme Court shall, if it considers that a question of public importance with reference to the enforcement of any of the Fundamental Rights conferred by Chapter 1 of Part II is involved, have the power to make an order of the nature mentioned in the said Article.

statutes.title: Qanun-e-Shahadat Order – Article 17 (Competency of Witnesses)
statutes.content: Article 17: Number of witnesses. (1) Unless otherwise provided in any law relating to the enforcement of Hudood or any other special law, in matters pertaining to financial or future obligations, if reduced to writing, the instrument shall be attested by two men, or one man and two women, so that one may remind the other, if necessary, and evidence shall be led accordingly.

statutes.title: Criminal Procedure Code – Section 265 (Trial by Sessions Court)
statutes.content: Section 265 CrPC: Whenever a person is triable by a Court of Session, that Court shall follow the procedure specified in Sections 265-A to 265-N, both inclusive.

statutes.title: Transfer of Property Act – Section 54 (Sale Defined)
statutes.content: Section 54 Transfer of Property Act: 'Sale' is a transfer of ownership in exchange for a price paid or promised or part-paid and part-promised. A contract for the sale of immoveable property is a contract that a sale of such property shall take place on terms settled between the parties. It does not, of itself, create any interest in or charge on such property.

statutes.title: West Pakistan Urban Rent Restriction Ordinance 1959
statutes.content: The West Pakistan Urban Rent Restriction Ordinance 1959 governs landlord-tenant relationships in urban areas. No landlord shall increase rent except by written notice and in accordance with provisions. Eviction only on specified grounds including non-payment, subletting without consent, or breach of material conditions.

statutes.title: Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961
statutes.content: The Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961 regulates matters of marriage, divorce, maintenance and dower. Section 7 provides for written notice of talaq to Chairman of Union Council. Section 9 deals with maintenance of wife. Section 10 deals with dower/mehr. Arbitration Council shall attempt reconciliation before divorce becomes effective.

statutes.title: Islamic Banking – Riba (Interest) Prohibition
statutes.content: The Federal Shariat Court has declared fixed interest (riba) as un-Islamic under Articles 203-B to 203-J of the Constitution. Under Shariah principles, financial transactions must be based on profit-sharing (Mudarabah), partnership (Musharakah), cost-plus financing (Murabaha), or leasing (Ijarah) rather than fixed interest rates.

statutes.title: National Accountability Bureau Ordinance 1999
statutes.content: The NAB Ordinance 1999 establishes accountability courts. Section 9 defines corruption and corrupt practices. Section 10 provides for penalties including imprisonment up to 14 years and fine. Plea bargaining allowed under Section 25-B. The burden of proof of innocence is on accused for unexplained assets.

statutes.title: Hudood Ordinances 1979
statutes.content: The Hudood Ordinances enforce Islamic criminal law. Offences of Zina (adultery), Qazf (false accusation of zina), alcohol consumption, and theft have prescribed Hadd punishments. Standard of proof for Hadd is very high – four witnesses for zina. If Hadd not proved, Tazir punishment can be awarded.

statutes.title: Companies Act 2017 – Section 192 (Directors Duties)
statutes.content: Section 192 Companies Act 2017: A director of a company shall act in good faith in order to promote the objects of the company for the benefit of its members as a whole, and in the best interests of the company, its employees, the shareholders, the community and for the protection of environment.

precedents.legal_principle: In cases of qatl-i-amd, ocular evidence must be corroborated by medical evidence. Mere presence at crime scene insufficient for conviction. Benefit of doubt must be given to accused where prosecution evidence is inconsistent.

precedents.case_title: Habib Bank Ltd v. Sugar Mills Ltd
precedents.legal_principle: Banking recovery suits – when borrower disputes loan amount, bank must produce complete statement of accounts duly reconciled. Unilateral bank statements without reconciliation insufficient to prove exact amount due.

precedents.case_title: Mst. Zainab Bibi v. Muhammad Usman
precedents.legal_principle: In khul' cases, wife's right to dissolution of marriage is absolute upon returning mehr. Court cannot force reconciliation against wife's will. Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961 must be read harmoniously with Quranic principles.

precedents.case_title: Messrs Fazal Ellahi v. Province of Punjab
precedents.legal_principle: Specific performance of contract for sale of land – time is not ordinarily the essence of contract in Pakistani law. However, if circumstances clearly indicate time was meant to be essential, delay defeats equity. Plaintiff must come with clean hands.

precedents.case_title: Karachi Electric Supply Corp v. Federation of Pakistan
precedents.legal_principle: Fixed interest on overdue bills constitutes riba and is repugnant to Islamic injunctions. Financial institutions must restructure penalty clauses on basis of actual loss or charitable donation (sadaqah) rather than pre-fixed interest.

precedents.case_title: Haji Abdul Wali v. Collector Land Acquisition
precedents.legal_principle: Compensation for compulsory acquisition of land must reflect market value at date of notification under Land Acquisition Act 1894. Solatium of 25% mandatory. Constitutional right of property protected under Article 23 cannot be defeated by inadequate compensation.

precedents.case_title: M/s Al-Ghazi Tractors v. Federal Board of Revenue
precedents.legal_principle: Tax assessment orders must be preceded by proper show cause notice and opportunity of hearing. Principles of natural justice are mandatory even in tax proceedings. Arbitrary assessments without evidence of concealment are void.

precedents.case_title: Balochistan Kidney Centre v. Government of Balochistan
precedents.legal_principle: Government's denial of essential healthcare facilities violates fundamental right to life under Article 9 of Constitution. Right to life encompasses right to basic medical care. Writ of mandamus lies against failure to provide statutory duty of care.

precedents.case_title: Muhammad Ali Mirza v. Election Commission of Pakistan
precedents.legal_principle: Right to vote and participate in elections is a fundamental right. Disqualification from contesting elections requires strict procedural compliance. Election tribunals must hear both parties before making disqualification orders.

precedents.case_title: Mian Muhammad Ashraf v. DHA Lahore
precedents.legal_principle: Housing society allotment letters create binding contractual obligations. Cancellation without following due process and without refund of full amount with profit constitutes breach of contract and unjust enrichment. Consumer protection principles apply.

legal_principles.application: No person should be condemned unheard. Fundamental principle of natural justice applied in all Pakistani courts.

legal_principles.translation: No one should be judge in their own case
legal_principles.application: Judges must be impartial and disclose conflicts of interest. Applicable in all judicial proceedings under Pakistan law.

legal_principles.translation: There shall be no harm and no reciprocation of harm
legal_principles.application: Islamic legal principle forming basis of tort law in Shariah. Applied in FSC and Shariat bench cases.

legal_principles.translation: Matters are determined by their objectives
legal_principles.application: Purpose and intent determines legal classification of acts. Applied in Islamic jurisprudence for contract classification.

legal_principles.maxim: Actus reus non facit reum nisi mens sit rea
legal_principles.translation: An act is not guilty unless the mind is also guilty
legal_principles.application: Guilty act must be accompanied by guilty mind. Applied in criminal cases under Pakistan Penal Code.
